title: Shadows of Brimstone - Part 7 - Five Things I love, Five Things I Don't
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Li2lFki33xM

Daniel (@dungeondive) steps back from the component deep dive to deliver a broad verdict on Shadows of Brimstone—five loves and five frustrations.

The loves: the genre-busting theme that throws Western, cosmic horror, sci-fi, WWII, and samurai into one pulpy stew (channelling Roland Deschain's philosophy that good stories are like stews with many flavours); the non-combat encounters that make every tile feel alive with narrative stat checks; the elegant character creation and levelling system; the staggering volume of gear and loot; and the satisfying core loop of dungeon, town, and back again that creates emergent campaigns without rigid scripting.

The frustrations: the core sets can feel incomplete without additional expansions, particularly in enemy variety; storage and organisation become a hobby unto themselves; the relentless d6 rolling can grow cumbersome; Flying Frog Productions has become essentially a single-game studio, neglecting Touch of Evil, Fortune and Glory, and Last Night on Earth; and the cost of entry is significant—this is a lifestyle game on par with Kingdom Death or Warhammer 40K in its demands on wallet and shelf space.

A measured take that makes clear why the game inspires devotion despite its rough edges.

----

Which of these five loves or five frustrations resonates most with your own experience?
